name: GIVE
tagline: Keep the relationship intact while you handle a hard conversation.
situations: A conversation where the relationship matters most
Wanting to disagree without damaging trust
Talking to someone you care about under stress
summary: GIVE is DBT's skill for preserving a relationship during a difficult interaction: be Gentle, act Interested, Validate, and use an Easy manner. Where DEAR MAN focuses on getting your objective, GIVE focuses on keeping the connection. The two are often used together so you can be both effective and kind.
howItWorks: People shut down or fight back when they feel attacked, dismissed, or disrespected — which derails even reasonable requests. GIVE lowers that defensiveness by communicating care and respect alongside whatever you're discussing. Validation in particular signals that you understand the other person's perspective, which makes them far more willing to stay engaged and hear yours.

steps.instruction: Gentle: no attacks, threats, or judgment — keep tone and words soft.
Interested: listen, make eye contact, don't interrupt; show you care what they think.
Validate: acknowledge their feelings and perspective as understandable.
steps.note: 'I can see why that felt unfair.'

steps.instruction: Easy manner: use a light touch, some warmth or humor; don't make it a battle.
steps.mantra: Soft on the person, clear on the issue.

whenToUse: When the relationship matters more than winning the point
Any sensitive talk with someone you care about
whenNotToUse: When you must hold a firm boundary that 'easy manner' would undercut
With someone exploiting your warmth to avoid accountability
When validation would mean endorsing harmful behavior
tips: Validation doesn't mean agreement — you can validate a feeling and still disagree
Pair with DEAR MAN to be both kind and clear
A little genuine warmth defuses a lot of tension
Listen more than you plan your reply

evidence.summary: GIVE is a DBT interpersonal-effectiveness skill; DBT has strong meta-analytic support, and validation is a well-supported component of effective communication.
citations: Linehan, M.M. (2015). DBT Skills Training Manual, 2nd ed. Guilford Press.
